Summary of what was done:

### 1. **New doc: [Subagents](/ai-tools/subagents)** (`ai-tools/subagents.mdx`)

Cursor-style subagents page, including:

- **What subagents are** — context isolation, parallel execution, specialized expertise, reusability  
- **How they work** — foreground vs background  
- **Built-in subagents** — Explore (codebase search), Bash (shell commands), Browser (MCP)  
- **Shell commands** — explicit note that **all shell commands require user approval** before running  
- **Search & infrastructure** — **grep search** (ripgrep-style) and **Vercel** (implemented)  
- **Expert queue** — handing off work to specialist subagents in order  
- **When to use subagents** vs main agent/skills  
- **Custom subagents** — file locations (`.summer/agents/`, `.cursor/agents/`), YAML frontmatter, config fields  
- **Using subagents** — automatic delegation, explicit invocation, parallel execution  
- **Example** — verifier subagent (full example)  
- **Best practices** and what to avoid  

### 2. **Updates to [AI Operations](/ai-tools/operations)** (`ai-tools/operations.mdx`)

New section **“Tools, search & execution”** with:

- **Shell commands require approval** — nothing runs until the user approves  
- **Grep search** — ripgrep-style codebase search  
- **Infrastructure (Vercel)** — implemented, EU where applicable  
- **Expert queue** — specialist subagents and ordered handoff  
- **Subagents** — short explanation and link to the new Subagents page  

A **Subagents** card was added to the “Getting started” card group at the bottom.

### 3. **Limitations** (`essentials/limitations.mdx`)

- Under **Tool calls**: “grep search across the codebase” and a bullet that **shell commands always require your approval** before any command runs.

### 4. **Navigation** (`docs.json`)

- **Subagents** added under “Using Summer” (after Project Intelligence, before Current Limitations).

I didn’t find the exact “last poll changes” file with syntax errors in the repo; all new and edited content is valid MDX/JSON and passes the linter. If you have the file or branch that had the syntax issues, point me to it and I can fix it.

                  "lineNumber": 7,
                  "content": "## What Are Subagents?"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Subagents are specialized AI assistants that Summer's agent can delegate work to. Each subagent runs in its own context, handles specific types of work, and returns results to the main agent. Use subagents to break down complex tasks, run work in parallel, and keep your main conversation focused."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "### Why Use Subagents?"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 15,
                  "content": "| **Context isolation** | Long research or exploration doesn't fill your main chat. Each subagent has its own context window. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 16,
                  "content": "| **Parallel execution** | Run multiple subagents at once. Work on different parts of your project without waiting for one to finish. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 17,
                  "content": "| **Specialized expertise** | Configure subagents with custom prompts and tools for domain-specific tasks (e.g. 3D, audio, debugging). |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 18,
                  "content": "| **Reusability** | Define custom subagents and reuse them across projects. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 20,
                  "content": "## How Subagents Work"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 22,
                  "content": "When the agent hits a complex task, it can start a subagent automatically. The subagent gets a prompt with the needed context, works on its own, and returns a final message with results."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 24,
                  "content": "Subagents start with a clean context. The parent agent includes relevant information in the prompt, since subagents do not see prior conversation history."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 30,
                  "content": "| **Foreground** | Blocks until the subagent completes. You get the result right away. | Sequential work where you need the output before continuing. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "| **Background** | Returns immediately. The subagent keeps working on its own. | Long-running tasks or running several workstreams in parallel. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 33,
                  "content": "## Built-in Subagents"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 35,
                  "content": "Summer includes built-in subagents that handle context-heavy work automatically. You do not need to configure them."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "| Subagent | Purpose | Why it's a subagent |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "| **Explore** | Searches and analyzes your codebase | Exploration produces a lot of intermediate output. Using a subagent keeps the main context clean and allows many parallel searches with a faster model. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 41,
                  "content": "| **Browser** | Controls the browser via MCP tools | Browser interactions produce noisy DOM snapshots and screenshots. The subagent filters this down to relevant results. |"

                {
                  "lineNumber": 54,
                  "content": "The **expert queue** lets the agent hand off work to specialist subagents in an ordered way.                 {
                  "lineNumber": 71,
                  "content": "If you find yourself creating a subagent for a simple, single-purpose task like \"generate a changelog\" or \"format imports,\" consider using a skill or slash command instead."

                  "content": "- **Keep subagents focused** — One clear responsibility per subagent. Avoid generic \"helper\" agents."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 185,
                  "content": "- **Invest in descriptions** — The &#96;description&#96; field drives when the agent delegates. Refine it and test with prompts to see which subagent runs."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 187,
                  "content": "- **Version control** — Commit &#96;.summer/agents/&#96; (or &#96;.cursor/agents/&#96;) so your team shares the same subagents."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 193,
                  "content": "- **Very long prompts** — Long prompts don't make the subagent smarter; they slow it down and are harder to maintain."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 194,
                  "content": "- **Too many subagents** — Start with 2–3 focused subagents. Add more only when you have clear, distinct use cases."

                  "content": "    description: &#96;Launch a subagent to handle a task autonomously. The subagent runs in a background queue and returns its findings/results when done."

                  "content": "If you can explore different areas in parallel, launch multiple subagents concurrently (up to 4 at a time). The orchestrator will wait for ALL subagents to complete before continuing, and receive all findings at once."

              "file": "../publicsummerengine/src/lib/ai/tools/experts/subagents/README.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1,
                  "content": "# Subagents"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 3,
                  "content": "Subagents are autonomous AI agents the orchestrator dispatches for delegated tasks. They run on Railway via the same BullMQ queue infrastructure as experts, but they present differently to the user."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 5,
                  "content": "## Subagents vs Experts"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 7,
                  "content": "The orchestrator has two ways to delegate work to background agents:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "- UI: \"Thought for 5s\" -- looks like the orchestrator's own internal thinking"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 12,
                  "content": "- Written in first person so users do not realize it is a separate agent"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 17,
                  "content": "**Subagents** (explore, generalPurpose):"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 21,
                  "content": "- Component: &#96;SubagentCard&#96; (&#96;components/tools/tool-subagent-card.tsx&#96;)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 22,
                  "content": "- No accept/reject (explore is read-only; GP writes are committed by the agent)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 25,
                  "content": "**Subagents differ from experts in message flow:**"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 26,
                  "content": "- Experts create a separate &#96;role: 'expert'&#96; message; subagents do not"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 27,
                  "content": "- Subagents render from a &#96;data-expert-consultation&#96; part inside the orchestrator's assistant message"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 29,
                  "content": "- Bridge parts (callEditorState, callEditorOps) are appended to the orchestrator message"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 30,
                  "content": "- Parallel subagents: orchestrator waits for ALL to complete before continuing"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "## Subagent Message Flow"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 36,
                  "content": "1. **Tool call** – Orchestrator calls &#96;subagent({ type, prompt, description })&#96;. Tool queues job, writes &#96;data-expert-consultation&#96; part (state: &#96;queued&#96;, jobId) to the orchestrator's stream, returns immediately."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 38,
                  "content": "3. **SSE expert-tool-output** – Tool parts are added to the consultation part's &#96;streamingParts&#96; array. SubagentCard uses bridge parts (EditorOps, EditorState) in a hidden div for dispatch. Expanded content shows only findings (one chat-style block). &#96;lastToolStatus&#96; subtitle updates (e.g. \"Reading file\", \"Searching codebase\")."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "4. **SSE expert-complete** – Consultation part updated with findings, filesFound, state: &#96;complete&#96;. If all subagents done, sends internal continuation message to trigger orchestrator."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 40,
                  "content": "5. **SSE expert-failed** – Same as complete but state: &#96;error&#96;; orchestrator still gets triggered with error summary."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 42,
                  "content": "The SubagentCard renders from the consultation part. No separate expert message is created for subagents."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 49,
                  "content": "- The worker saves a complete expert message to DB when the subagent finishes"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 54,
                  "content": "1. Build a map of expert messages by &#96;metadata.jobId&#96; (worker saves these with &#96;role: 'expert'&#96;, &#96;metadata.subagent&#96;, &#96;metadata.jobId&#96;)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 57,
                  "content": "4. Filter out expert messages from the final display list (they are used only for hydration; SubagentCard renders from the consultation part)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 61,
                  "content": "## Subagent Types"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 67,
                  "content": "- **Modes**: &#96;fast&#96; (default) or &#96;inherit&#96;. Same prompt for both (per Cursor); only the model changes."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 69,
                  "content": "  - &#96;inherit&#96;: orchestrator's selected model (e.g. Opus 4.6)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 77,
                  "content": "Full-capability agent with read and write access."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 79,
                  "content": "- **Modes**: &#96;fast&#96; or &#96;inherit&#96;. Different prompts and models (per Cursor)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 80,
                  "content": "  - &#96;fast&#96;: Composer-style prompt, &#96;google/gemini-3-flash&#96;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 81,
                  "content": "  - &#96;inherit&#96;: Parent-style prompt (full citing_code, tone_and_style, etc.), orchestrator's model"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 86,
                  "content": "## How the Orchestrator Uses Subagents"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 88,
                  "content": "The orchestrator has a single &#96;subagent&#96; tool. It chooses the type based on the task:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 91,
                  "content": "subagent({"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 93,
                  "content": "  prompt: \"How does the health system work? Look at scripts and signals.\","
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 98,
                  "content": "subagent({"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 100,
                  "content": "  prompt: \"Refactor player.gd to use a state machine pattern.\","
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 105,
                  "content": "The orchestrator can launch multiple subagents in parallel (e.g. explore different parts of the codebase simultaneously). Each one runs independently on Railway."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 107,
                  "content": "When a subagent completes, the orchestrator receives a summary via the continuation system (&#96;use-expert-continuation.ts&#96;). The orchestrator sees only the final text -- not intermediate tool calls or reasoning steps."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 112,
                  "content": "                    Orchestrator (Vercel)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 114,
                  "content": "          [expert tools]          [subagent tool]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 141,
                  "content": "   ExpertConsultation       SubagentCard"

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 187,
                  "content": "Subagent costs are tracked through the same Vercel AI Gateway billing as all other AI calls. The cost calculator (&#96;src/lib/ai/cost-calculator.ts&#96;) has entries for all models used:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 197,

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" We reverse-engineered Cursor's plan mode extensively and have detailed documentation of exactly how it works: the AI writes a plan, it's stored as a markdown file with YAML frontmatter, the user reviews it, clicks \"Build\" to execute, and the AI implements the plan. The full Cursor plan mode analysis is in &#96;Docs/prompt_test/plan_mode/&#96; — read both files there carefully:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/plan_mode/plan_mode_injection.md&#96; — What gets injected when plan mode activates (first turn: full 12 rules + mermaid syntax, subsequent turns: short reminder), how the CreatePlan tool works, what Build does (switches to Agent mode, attaches plan body), how todos sync"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 12,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/plan_mode/full_context_flow_and_findings.md&#96; — The complete flow tested step-by-step: plan creation → storage → review → Build → execution. Key findings: plan stored as markdown with YAML frontmatter (name, overview, todos, isProject), plan body attached to Build message as code selection (frontmatter stripped), same conversation thread continues after Build"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 69,
                  "content": "**Handled by:** Orchestrator directly (code writing, not scene creation)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 125,
                  "content": "- Modes: 'agent' (default), 'plan', 'debug', 'ask'"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 126,
                  "content": "- When mode is 'plan', the system_reminder injection from W1's &#96;prompts/modes/plan.ts&#96; is included"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 132,
                  "content": "- route.ts (W6) passes the mode to buildSystemPrompt()"
                },

                  "lineNumber": 133,

                  "lineNumber": 145,

                },

                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" &#96;route.ts&#96; is the central nervous system — the POST handler where user messages arrive, the system prompt is assembled, tools are registered, the AI model is called via &#96;streamText()&#96;, and the response streams back. Everything connects here."

This way the code compiles and the integration points are documented."
                },

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "You are working on the Summer Engine web repo (publicsummerengine). Your job is to wire EVERYTHING together in &#96;app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts&#96; — the central file that connects the AI to all tools, prompts, and systems."

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 236,

            {
              "file": "../publicsummerengine/Docs/prompt_test/cloud_agents_started_in_development/W5_expert_streaming_ui.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" The chat interface is a Next.js React app. When the AI delegates work to expert agents (scene creation, 2D scene building), the expert runs in the background for 30-60 seconds on a Railway worker and streams status updates via SSE. Currently this output appears as free-form text in the chat — tool calls, operations, reasoning all mixed together. It's hard to follow and looks unprofessional."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "We're also building a new research agent (agent W2) that runs 2-5 second codebase scans and can run multiple in parallel. When 3 research queries run simultaneously, the user needs to see 3 progress indicators."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 27,
                  "content": "You are working on the Summer Engine web repo (publicsummerengine). Your job is to create a new React UI component that displays expert and research agent work in the chat as collapsible containers."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "Build a UI component that renders in the chat when experts or research agents are working. Currently, expert output streams as free-form text in the chat — it looks messy and unfocused. You're building the polished version."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "2. **Research agents** (being built by W2) — Fast (2-5s), return results inline"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 53,
                  "content": "A collapsible container that renders in the chat message stream when an expert or research agent is active."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 74,
                  "content": "### Research Agent Container Component"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 112,
                  "content": "**Research agent (new, being defined by W2):**"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 140,
                  "content": "- Do NOT modify any AI/tool/prompt files (W1-W4 handle those)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 142,
                  "content": "- Do NOT modify expert agent files (scene-expert, 2d-expert)"
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "../publicsummerengine/Docs/prompt_test/cloud_agents_started_in_development/W4_rules_and_ignore_system.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "From our Cursor analysis: Cursor has 3 layers of user rules — global settings, &#96;.cursorrules&#96; file at project root, and &#96;.cursor/rules/*.mdc&#96; files for granular rules. All are injected into the AI's context as &#96;&lt;rules&gt;&#96; / &#96;&lt;user_rules&gt;&#96; tags in every message. See &#96;Docs/prompt_test/master_system_prompt.md&#96; Layer 3 for how Cursor injects them. Important finding: Cursor's subagents do NOT receive user rules — only the main agent does."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 15,
                  "content": "- A formatted output that W1's &#96;buildSystemPrompt()&#96; can inject into the prompt"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 21,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/07_RULES_SYSTEM.md&#96; — Full spec"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 22,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/master_system_prompt.md&#96; — How Cursor injects rules (Layer 3)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 23,
                  "content": "- Agent D2 handles the C++ side (.summerignore pattern matching in SearchInFiles/fs_tree_state)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 24,
                  "content": "- Agent W1 handles the prompt injection point (expects a formatted string from you)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 32,
                  "content": "Create the rules loading system that reads user-configurable AI instructions from the project and injects them into the orchestrator prompt. Also create the web-side .summerignore handling."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 38,
                  "content": "The files live in the user's Godot project (on their desktop, accessed via the bridge). The web server needs to read them via the bridge and inject the content into the AI prompt."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 41,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/07_RULES_SYSTEM.md&#96; — Full spec"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 42,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/master_system_prompt.md&#96; — How Cursor injects rules (Layer 3 per-message context)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 45,
                  "content": "- &#96;app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts&#96; — Where the prompt is assembled (to understand where rules will be injected)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 88,
                  "content": "5. **Format for prompt injection** — Return a formatted string ready to insert into the system prompt:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 123,
                  "content": "If the C++ side (D2 agent) hasn't implemented .summerignore yet, the web side needs a fallback:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 156,
                  "content": "This string gets passed to W1's &#96;buildSystemPrompt()&#96; as the &#96;projectContext&#96; parameter, which wraps it in &#96;&lt;project_context&gt;&#96; tags."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 168,
                  "content": "- Do NOT modify &#96;prompts.ts&#96; or the prompts directory (W1 handles the injection point)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 178,
                  "content": "4. **The formatted output must work with W1's buildSystemPrompt.** It expects a string that gets wrapped in &#96;&lt;project_rules&gt;&#96; tags. Just return the content, W1's index.ts adds the tags."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "../publicsummerengine/Docs/prompt_test/cloud_agents_started_in_development/W3_new_tool_wrappers.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" The AI orchestrator (Next.js/Vercel AI SDK) talks to the desktop Godot editor through a WebView native bridge. Each tool on the web side: (1) defines a Zod schema + description via the AI SDK's &#96;tool()&#96; function, (2) writes a JSON bridge message via &#96;dataStream.write()&#96;, (3) waits for the desktop result via &#96;waitForBridgeResult()&#96;, and (4) returns the result to the model."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "Agent D1 is creating new C++ operations on the desktop side (shell execution, viewport screenshots, code intelligence, animation, game state, project management). You're creating the TypeScript wrappers that expose these operations to the AI model. Every new C++ operation needs a web-side tool wrapper."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "content": "**The tool description is critical.** From our Cursor analysis: Cursor's tool descriptions are LONG and specific — they include examples, explain when to use vs when NOT to use, and describe what the return value means. The AI reads the description to decide whether to call the tool and how. A vague description like \"Execute a command\" means the AI won't know when it's appropriate. A good description like Cursor's Shell tool (which embeds git safety protocols, working directory behavior, and background execution patterns directly in the description) makes the AI use it correctly. See &#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/&#96; for Cursor's tool descriptions as reference."

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1,
                  "content": "# W2 — Research Agent + Parallel Agent Infrastructure"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 3,
                  "content": "The title of this chat is \"W2 Research Agent + Parallel Infra — Summer Engine Fast Research\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" The orchestrator currently delegates complex work to expert agents (scene, 2D) that run on Railway workers via BullMQ queues. These are HEAVY — 30-60 seconds per call. There is no lightweight option for quick codebase exploration. When the orchestrator needs to understand what exists in the user's project before making a decision, it has no efficient way to do it."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "We reverse-engineered Cursor and found it has two subagent types: &#96;explore&#96; (fast, read-only, 9 tools, returns in 2-5 seconds) and &#96;generalPurpose&#96; (full tool set, heavier). The explore agent uses a cheaper model and is sandboxed — it can search, read, and list files but cannot write. It runs INLINE (direct API call, not queued) so results come back in the same turn. See &#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/task_management/subagents/&#96; for the full Cursor subagent analysis, including the discovery that explore agents get a completely different system prompt (\"Composer\" identity, different tool names)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "content": "**What you're building:** A research agent that runs inline on Vercel (NOT through BullMQ/Railway). It uses &#96;generateText()&#96; with a fast/cheap model, has read-only tools (searchInFiles, readFile, listTree, callEditorState), and returns findings to the orchestrator in the same turn. The orchestrator calls it before expert delegation to gather project context, or when the user asks \"where is X?\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 15,
                  "content": "**The hard design problem:** The existing tools (searchInFiles, readFile, etc.) are designed for the streaming architecture — they write to a &#96;dataStream&#96; and use &#96;waitForBridgeResult()&#96;. The research agent runs INSIDE a tool's execute function, so it needs a different way to access the bridge. Study &#96;src/lib/bridge/waiter.ts&#96; and figure out the best approach. This is the core challenge."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 17,
                  "content": "**You're also building parallel execution support:** The orchestrator should be able to fire 2-3 research queries simultaneously (Promise.all). When parallel queries complete, results need to be combined into one context block. Design the data stream messages for the frontend to show research progress (agent W5 is building the UI component for this)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 20,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/05_AGENT_ARCHITECTURE.md&#96; — Agent hierarchy design"

                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/task_management/subagents/README.md&#96; — Full subagent architecture comparison"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 26,
                  "content": "You are working on the Summer Engine web repo (publicsummerengine). Your job is to build a fast, lightweight research agent that the orchestrator can call to explore the user's game project, AND the infrastructure for running multiple research agents in parallel."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 30,
                  "content": "Build the research agent system:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "1. A lightweight agent that uses a fast/cheap model to search the codebase"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 32,
                  "content": "2. A tool wrapper so the orchestrator can call it"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "4. Design for how results get combined and returned to the orchestrator"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 38,
                  "content": "Currently, the orchestrator delegates complex work to experts (scene, 2D) via BullMQ → Railway workers. These are SLOW (30-60 seconds). The research agent is different: it's FAST (2-5 seconds), runs INLINE on Vercel (not queued), and is READ-ONLY."

                {

                  "content": "The research agent is the equivalent of Cursor's \"explore\" subagent — a quick codebase scanner that runs before expert delegation to gather context."

                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" The AI orchestrator runs on Next.js with Vercel AI SDK 6.                   "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine built on Next.js + Vercel AI SDK. The &#96;ai&#96; package is one of the most critical dependencies — it handles all LLM communication, tool calling, and streaming. 8 other agents are implementing features in parallel and they ALL depend on the SDK working correctly. Three agents specifically need your findings:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "**W1 (prompt rewrite)** needs to know: How does Anthropic prompt caching work with the SDK?               "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \"Cursor for game development.\" It's a fork of Godot 4.x where an AI orchestrator (Next.js on Vercel) controls the desktop Godot editor through a WebView native bridge. The AI sends JSON operation messages through the bridge, the desktop executes them via &#96;OpsExecutor&#96;, and returns results."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "We reverse-engineered Cursor's full agent architecture — its system prompt, 52+ tools, 4 modes, subagent system — and identified critical gaps in Summer. The AI currently can't run shell commands, see what it built (no screenshots), extract code structure, create animations programmatically, or check game performance. Agent D1 is building all the new C++ operation files to close these gaps. Your job is two things:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 18,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&#96; — Complete operation specs (what D1 is creating)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 19,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/07_RULES_SYSTEM.md&#96; — The .summerignore spec"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 20,
                  "content": "- &#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/01_MASTER_PLAN.md&#96; — Why all of this matters"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 60,
                  "content": "Wire all new operations from the D1 agent into the main dispatch."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 129,
                  "content": "2. **The operation function signatures must match what D1 creates.** If D1's headers don't exist yet (because agents run in parallel), use the naming convention: &#96;op_snake_case_name(const Dictionary &amp;op)&#96; returning &#96;Dictionary&#96;. The exact function names are listed above."
                }

              "matches": [

                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — essentially \"Cursor for game development.\" It's a fork of Godot 4.x with an AI orchestrator that helps users build complete games through conversation. The AI talks to the user via a web interface (Next.js on Vercel), and executes operations on the user's desktop Godot editor through a WebView native bridge."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "We recently conducted an exhaustive analysis of how Cursor (the AI code editor) works internally — reverse-engineering its full system prompt, all 52+ tool definitions, its 4 modes (Agent, Plan, Debug, Ask), its subagent architecture, and how context flows between components. This analysis is documented in &#96;Docs/prompt_test/&#96; in both Summer repos."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 23,
                  "content": "This agent's job is to build ALL the missing C++ operations in one engine build, so the web side can iterate rapidly afterward (web deploys are minutes on Vercel; engine builds are hours of compile + bundle + ship)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 27,
                  "content": "Create new C++ operation files in &#96;modules/1summer_engine/editor/ops/&#96;. Each file implements operations that the AI orchestrator will call via the WebView bridge."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 45,
                  "content": "Read these docs (they're in both repos under &#96;Docs/prompt_test/&#96;):"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 47,
                  "content": "8. **&#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&#96;** — Complete specification for every operation you need to create. This is your primary requirements document."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 48,
                  "content": "9. **&#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/01_MASTER_PLAN.md&#96;** — The high-level vision. Understand WHY these operations matter."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 49,
                  "content": "10. **&#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/03_ANIMATION_RD.md&#96;** — Animation-specific R&amp;D context. The animation operations are experimental."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 53,
                  "content": "11. **&#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/terminal/shell.md&#96;** — How Cursor's Shell tool works (for reference when building RunCommand). Note the patterns: stateful shell, background execution, working directory support."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 54,
                  "content": "12. **&#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/code_quality/read_lints.md&#96;** — How Cursor's ReadLints tool works (for reference when building GetScriptErrors)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 55,
                  "content": "13. **&#96;Docs/prompt_test/OVERVIEW.md&#96;** — Navigation guide to all the Cursor analysis docs. Browse if you need more context."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 426,
                  "content": "3. **Do NOT modify ops_executor.cpp or ops_executor.h.** Agent D2 handles dispatch registration."

              "file": "../publicsummerengine/Docs/prompt_test/tools_task_and_management.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10,
                  "content": "Launch a new agent to handle complex, multi-step tasks autonomously."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 12,
                  "content": "The Task tool launches specialized subagents (subprocesses) that autonomously handle complex tasks. Each subagent_type has specific capabilities and tools available to it."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 14,
                  "content": "When using the Task tool, you must specify a subagent_type parameter to select which agent type to use."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 16,
                  "content": "VERY IMPORTANT: When exploring the codebase to gather context or to answer a question that is not a needle query for a specific file/class/function, it is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED that you use the Task tool with subagent_type=\"explore\" instead of running search commands directly."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 20,
                  "content": "- user: \"How does authentication work in this codebase?\" assistant: [Uses the Task tool with subagent_type=\"explore\" - this requires exploring multiple files to understand the auth flow]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 21,
                  "content": "- user: \"What is the codebase structure?\" assistant: [Uses the Task tool with subagent_type=\"explore\"]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 23,
                  "content": "If it is possible to explore different areas of the codebase in parallel, you should launch multiple agents concurrently."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 26,
                  "content": "- Simple, single or few-step tasks that can be performed by a single agent (using parallel or sequential tool calls) -- just call the tools directly instead."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 33,
                  "content": "- Always include a short description (3-5 words) summarizing what the agent will do"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "- Launch multiple agents concurrently whenever possible, to maximize performance; to do that, use a single message with multiple tool uses. IMPORTANT: DO NOT launch more than 4 agents concurrently."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 35,
                  "content": "- When the agent is done, it will return a single message back to you. Specify exactly what information the agent should return back in its final response to you. The result returned by the agent is not visible to the user. To show the user the result, you should send a text message back to the user with a concise summary of the result."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 36,
                  "content": "- Agents can be resumed using the &#96;resume&#96; parameter by passing the agent ID from a previous invocation. When resumed, the agent continues with its full previous context preserved. When NOT resuming, each invocation starts fresh and you should provide a detailed task description with all necessary context."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "- When using the Task tool, the subagent invocation does not have access to the user's message or prior assistant steps. Therefore, you should provide a highly detailed task description with all necessary context for the agent to perform its task autonomously."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 38,
                  "content": "- The subagent's outputs should generally be trusted"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "- Clearly tell the subagent which tasks you want it to perform, since it is not aware of the user's intent or your prior assistant steps (tool calls, thinking, or messages)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 40,
                  "content": "- If the subagent description mentions that it should be used proactively, then you should try your best to use it without the user having to ask for it first. Use your judgement."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 41,
                  "content": "- If the user specifies that they want you to run subagents \"in parallel\", you MUST send a single message with multiple Task tool use content blocks."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 43,
                  "content": "Available subagent_types and a quick description of what they do:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 44,
                  "content": "- generalPurpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. Use when searching for a keyword or file and not confident you'll find the match quickly."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 45,
                  "content": "- explore: Fast agent specialized for exploring codebases. Use this when you need to quickly find files by patterns (eg. \"src/components/**/*.tsx\"), search code for keywords (eg. \"API endpoints\"), or answer questions about the codebase (eg. \"how do API endpoints work?\"). When calling this agent, specify the desired thoroughness level: \"quick\" for basic searches, \"medium\" for moderate exploration, or \"very thorough\" for comprehensive analysis across multiple locations and naming conventions."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 50,
                  "content": "When speaking to the USER about which model you selected for a Task/subagent, do NOT reveal these internal model alias names (e.g., fast, alpha, beta, gamma). Instead, use natural language such as \"a faster model\", \"a more capable model\", or \"the default model\"."

                  "lineNumber": 26,

                  "lineNumber": 28,

                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "These are **completely different sections** from the parent's (tone_and_style, citing_code, inline_line_numbers, terminal_files_information, etc.). The parent's detailed citing_code rules, terminal file info, and mode_selection sections do NOT exist in the explore agent's prompt."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "The explore agent sees 9 tools with DIFFERENT names:"
                },

                  "lineNumber": 51,

                  "lineNumber": 77,

                },

                  "lineNumber": 79,

                  "lineNumber": 82,

                  "lineNumber": 86,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 87,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 91,
                  "content": "## Critical finding: generalPurpose (fast) gets the EXPLORE prompt"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 93,
                  "content": "The &#96;generalPurpose&#96; subagent with &#96;model=\"fast\"&#96; reported a system prompt more similar to the **explore** agents — the \"Composer\" identity, different tool names, etc. It seems that the &#96;fast&#96; model may route to the explore/Composer infrastructure regardless of the subagent_type parameter, or the fast model gets a simplified prompt."
                },

                  "lineNumber": 95,
                  "content": "This is unexpected — you'd think &#96;generalPurpose + fast&#96; would be a fast version of the full agent, but instead it appears to be more like an explore agent."

                  "lineNumber": 101,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 105,
                  "content": "| agent_skills | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 112,
                  "content": "**Notable:** Subagents get a **current** git_status (showing all our recent modifications and untracked files), not the conversation-start snapshot the parent has. This makes sense — they're spawned fresh."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 138,
                  "content": "1. **Two system prompt architectures**: The &#96;fast&#96; model gets the \"Composer\" prompt (identity: \"Composer, a language model trained by Cursor\"). The inherited model gets the parent's prompt (identity: \"claude-4.6-opus-high-thinking\"). This is determined by MODEL, not subagent_type."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 140,
                  "content": "2. **subagent_type determines tool scope**: &#96;explore&#96; = 9 read-only tools + read-only system_reminder. &#96;generalPurpose&#96; = full tool set (~50 tools) with write access. This is independent of model."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 143,
                  "content": "   - explore + fast = Composer prompt, 9 tools, read-only"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 144,
                  "content": "   - explore + inherit = Composer prompt, 9 tools, read-only"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 145,
                  "content": "   - GP + fast = Composer prompt, ~50 tools, full write access"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 146,
                  "content": "   - GP + inherit = Parent prompt, 52 tools, full write access (no Task tool)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 148,
                  "content": "4. **Subagents cannot spawn sub-subagents**: No subagent had the Task tool available. Delegation is one level deep."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 150,
                  "content": "5. **Subagents have no awareness of being subagents**: The GP (inherit) agent explicitly stated it had \"NO indication\" of being spawned by a parent."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 152,
                  "content": "6. **Subagents don't get open files or conversation history**: They start fresh with only the prompt, workspace context, and git status."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 154,
                  "content": "7. **Subagents get current git status**: Unlike the parent (snapshot from conversation start), subagents see the actual current state."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 156,
                  "content": "8. **Explore agents are heavily sandboxed**: Read-only, 9 tools, \"file search specialist\" system_reminder, performance-optimized."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 158,
                  "content": "9. **GP fast has the Composer prompt BUT full write tools**: Including delete_file, search_replace, write, all browser tools, MCP tools, etc. So the \"Composer\" prompt is not inherently read-only — the explore system_reminder enforces that."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 160,
                  "content": "10. **More capable models follow \"don't disclose\" better**: GP (inherit on Opus 4.6) refused to dump its system prompt. Fast models and explore agents complied."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 162,
                  "content": "11. **Tool name differences between architectures**: Fast/Composer agents use &#96;read_file&#96;, &#96;search_replace&#96;, &#96;run_terminal_cmd&#96;, &#96;codebase_search&#96;. Inherited/Parent agents use &#96;Read&#96;, &#96;StrReplace&#96;, &#96;Shell&#96;, &#96;SemanticSearch&#96;. Same functionality, different names."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 164,
                  "content": "12. **GP fast has &#96;&lt;think&gt;&#96; tag support**: The Composer prompt includes \"You can use &lt;think&gt; tags to think through problems step by step.\" This is NOT in the parent agent's prompt."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 166,
                  "content": "13. **The parent only sees the final text**: Confirmed — none of the subagents' tool calls, reasoning, or intermediate steps are visible to the parent."
                }
              ]
            },

                  "content": "Launch a new agent to handle complex, multi-step tasks autonomously."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 8,
                  "content": "The Task tool launches specialized subagents (subprocesses) that autonomously handle complex tasks. Each subagent_type has specific capabilities and tools available to it."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10,
                  "content": "When using the Task tool, you must specify a subagent_type parameter to select which agent type to use."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 12,
                  "content": "VERY IMPORTANT: When exploring the codebase to gather context or to answer a question that is not a needle query for a specific file/class/function, it is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED that you use the Task tool with subagent_type=\"explore\" instead of running search commands directly."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 16,
                  "content": "- user: \"How does authentication work in this codebase?\" assistant: [Uses the Task tool with subagent_type=\"explore\" - this requires exploring multiple files to understand the auth flow]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 17,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 19,
                  "content": "If it is possible to explore different areas of the codebase in parallel, you should launch multiple agents concurrently."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 22,
                  "content": "- Simple, single or few-step tasks that can be performed by a single agent (using parallel or sequential tool calls) -- just call the tools directly instead."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 29,
                  "content": "- Always include a short description (3-5 words) summarizing what the agent will do"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 30,
                  "content": "- Launch multiple agents concurrently whenever possible, to maximize performance; to do that, use a single message with multiple tool uses. IMPORTANT: DO NOT launch more than 4 agents concurrently."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "- When the agent is done, it will return a single message back to you. Specify exactly what information the agent should return back in its final response to you. The result returned by the agent is not visible to the user. To show the user the result, you should send a text message back to the user with a concise summary of the result."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 32,
                  "content": "- Agents can be resumed using the &#96;resume&#96; parameter by passing the agent ID from a previous invocation. When resumed, the agent continues with its full previous context preserved. When NOT resuming, each invocation starts fresh and you should provide a detailed task description with all necessary context."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 33,
                  "content": "- When using the Task tool, the subagent invocation does not have access to the user's message or prior assistant steps. Therefore, you should provide a highly detailed task description with all necessary context for the agent to perform its task autonomously."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "- The subagent's outputs should generally be trusted"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 35,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 36,

                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "Available subagent_types and a quick description of what they do:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 40,
                  "content": "- generalPurpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. Use when searching for a keyword or file and not confident you'll find the match quickly."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 41,
                  "content": "- explore: Fast agent specialized for exploring codebases. Use this when you need to quickly find files by patterns (eg. \"src/components/**/*.tsx\"), search code for keywords (eg. \"API endpoints\"), or answer questions about the codebase (eg. \"how do API endpoints work?\"). When calling this agent, specify the desired thoroughness level: \"quick\" for basic searches, \"medium\" for moderate exploration, or \"very thorough\" for comprehensive analysis across multiple locations and naming conventions."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 46,

                {
                  "lineNumber": 233,
                  "content": "## Scene Composition Strategy"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 252,
                  "content": "  {op: \"AddNode\", type: \"CharacterBody3D\", name: \"Player\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 253,
                  "content": "  {op: \"AddNode\", parent: \"./Player\", type: \"MeshInstance3D\", name: \"Body\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 256,
                  "content": "  {op: \"SaveScene\", path: \"res://scenes/player.tscn\"}"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 261,
                  "content": "  {op: \"InstantiateScene\", parent: \"./World\", scene: \"res://scenes/player.tscn\", name: \"Player1\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 264,
                  "content": "  {op: \"InstantiateScene\", parent: \"./World\", scene: \"res://scenes/player.tscn\", name: \"Player2\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 290,
                  "content": "    content: \\&#96;extends CharacterBody3D"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 352,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"MeshInstance3D\", name: \"Box\"}"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 355,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"MeshInstance3D\", name: \"Box\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 372,
                  "content": "parent: \"/\"              // CORRECT - Scene root (use this for first-level children)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 391,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"MeshInstance3D\", name: \"Box\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 397,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"MeshInstance3D\", name: \"Box\"}  // Box doesn't exist yet!"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 402,
                  "content": "### Essential 3D Nodes"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 404,
                  "content": "Node3D              - Base spatial node (use for containers)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 405,
                  "content": "DirectionalLight3D  - Sun-like parallel light (outdoor scenes)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 406,
                  "content": "OmniLight3D         - Point light (indoor/localized)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 407,
                  "content": "SpotLight3D         - Cone light (flashlight, stage lights)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 409,
                  "content": "Camera3D            - View frustum (use current=true for active camera)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 411,
                  "content": "MeshInstance3D      - Visual geometry (needs mesh resource)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 412,
                  "content": "StaticBody3D        - Static collision (for terrain, walls)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 413,
                  "content": "CharacterBody3D     - Player/NPC physics"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 414,
                  "content": "RigidBody3D         - Dynamic physics objects"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 415,
                  "content": "CollisionShape3D    - Collision volume (needs shape resource)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 417,
                  "content": "Node2D              - Base 2D node"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 418,
                  "content": "Sprite2D            - 2D images"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 432,
                  "content": "BoxShape3D          - Box collision (default 1×1×1)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 433,
                  "content": "SphereShape3D       - Sphere collision (default radius 0.5)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 434,
                  "content": "CapsuleShape3D      - Capsule collision (default radius 0.5, height 2)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 435,
                  "content": "CylinderShape3D     - Cylinder collision"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 438,
                  "content": "## Complete Scene Example"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 440,
                  "content": "Request: \"Create 3D outdoor scene with platform\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 446,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"/\", \"type\": \"Node3D\", \"name\": \"World\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 447,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World\", \"type\": \"DirectionalLight3D\", \"name\": \"Sun\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 454,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World\", \"type\": \"StaticBody3D\", \"name\": \"Ground\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 455,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World/Ground\", \"type\": \"MeshInstance3D\", \"name\": \"Mesh\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 458,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World/Ground\", \"type\": \"CollisionShape3D\", \"name\": \"Collision\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 459,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"SetProp\", \"path\": \"./World/Ground/Collision\", \"key\": \"shape\", \"value\": \"BoxShape3D\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 461,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World\", \"type\": \"Camera3D\", \"name\": \"Camera\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 464,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"SaveScene\", \"path\": \"res://main.tscn\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 467,
                  "content": "  \"reasoning\": \"I need DirectionalLight3D positioned above at (0,10,0) rotated -45° for natural sun angle. Ground uses StaticBody3D hierarchy with BoxMesh visual (20×0.5×20 platform) and matching BoxShape3D collision. Camera positioned at 10° elevation looking at origin. Environment resource provides ambient lighting fallback.\","
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 468,
                  "content": "  \"assumptions\": \"Outdoor scene uses DirectionalLight3D (not OmniLight3D). Platform is static (StaticBody3D, not RigidBody3D). Collision dimensions match visual mesh. All rotations in radians. Resources use defaults then customize via SetResourceProperty.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 474,
                  "content": "**Scene Operations:**"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 480,
                  "content": "- \\&#96;SaveScene\\&#96; - Save current scene to disk"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 481,
                  "content": "- \\&#96;InstantiateScene\\&#96; - Instance a .tscn file as child node"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 517,
                  "content": "- Think in hierarchies (Node3D → children)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 628,
                  "content": "      { op: \"AddNode\", type: \"CharacterBody3D\", name: \"Player\", parent: \"/\" },"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 638,
                  "content": "// Create Platform with children (MeshInstance3D and CollisionShape3D)"

                  "lineNumber": 641,

                },
                {

                },
                {

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 649,
                  "content": "  { op: \"SetProp\", path: \"/Platform/PlatformCollision\", key: \"shape\", value: \"BoxShape3D\" }"

                {

                {

                  "lineNumber": 169,

                {
                  "lineNumber": 170,

                {

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 190,
                  "content": "## Scene Composition Strategy"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 206,
                  "content": "  {op: \"AddNode\", type: \"CharacterBody2D\", name: \"Player\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 210,
                  "content": "  {op: \"SaveScene\", path: \"res://scenes/player.tscn\"}"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 215,
                  "content": "  {op: \"InstantiateScene\", parent: \"./World\", scene: \"res://scenes/player.tscn\", name: \"Player1\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 218,
                  "content": "  {op: \"InstantiateScene\", parent: \"./World\", scene: \"res://scenes/player.tscn\", name: \"Player2\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 228,
                  "content": "## Essential 2D Nodes"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 230,
                  "content": "### Core 2D Nodes:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 231,
                  "content": "Node2D               - Base 2D spatial node (use as root container)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 232,
                  "content": "Sprite2D             - 2D sprite (display texture/image)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 234,
                  "content": "Polygon2D            - Polygonal shapes"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 235,
                  "content": "AnimatedSprite2D     - Animated 2D sprite"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 237,
                  "content": "Camera2D             - 2D camera (use current=true for active camera)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 239,
                  "content": "### 2D Physics Bodies:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 240,
                  "content": "StaticBody2D         - Static collision (for platforms, walls, ground)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 241,
                  "content": "CharacterBody2D      - Player/NPC physics with move_and_slide()"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 242,
                  "content": "RigidBody2D          - Dynamic physics objects (falling, bouncing)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 243,
                  "content": "Area2D               - Detection zones, pickups, triggers"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 245,
                  "content": "### Collision Shapes (2D):"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 246,
                  "content": "CollisionShape2D     - Collision volume (needs shape resource)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 247,
                  "content": "RectangleShape2D     - Rectangle collision"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 248,
                  "content": "CircleShape2D        - Circle collision"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 249,
                  "content": "CapsuleShape2D       - Capsule collision (vertical or horizontal)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 250,
                  "content": "SegmentShape2D       - Line segment"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 252,
                  "content": "### Important 2D Concepts:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 254,
                  "content": "1. Y-Axis Orientation: In 2D, Y increases DOWNWARD (top of screen is y=0). This is opposite of typical math coordinates."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 256,
                  "content": "2. Sprite2D vs ColorRect:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 258,
                  "content": "   - Sprite2D: Requires texture/image file. Use for final assets. Set texture property to \"res://sprites/player.png\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 260,
                  "content": "3. AnimatedSprite2D: For spritesheet animations. Requires SpriteFrames resource with animation frames."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 262,
                  "content": "4. Physics Bodies Hierarchy: Always add CollisionShape2D as a CHILD of the physics body (StaticBody2D, CharacterBody2D, RigidBody2D)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 264,
                  "content": "5. Collision Shapes Must Match Visual Size: If ColorRect is 800×20, the RectangleShape2D must also be 800×20."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 274,
                  "content": "Example: 2D Player Controller with Arrow Keys"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 280,
                  "content": "    content: \"extends CharacterBody2D\\n\\nconst SPEED = 200.0\\nconst JUMP_VELOCITY = -400.0\\n\\nfunc _physics_process(delta):\\n    if not is_on_floor():\\n        velocity += get_gravity() * delta\\n    \\n    if Input.is_action_just_pressed(\"ui_accept\") and is_on_floor():\\n        velocity.y = JUMP_VELOCITY\\n    \\n    var direction = Input.get_axis(\"ui_left\", \"ui_right\")\\n    if direction:\\n        velocity.x = direction * SPEED\\n    else:\\n        velocity.x = move_toward(velocity.x, 0, SPEED)\\n    \\n    move_and_slide()\\n\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 301,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"Sprite2D\", name: \"PlayerSprite\"}"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 309,
                  "content": "parent: \"/\"              // CORRECT - Scene root"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 318,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"CharacterBody2D\", name: \"Player\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 319,
                  "content": "{op: \"AddNode\", type: \"CollisionShape2D\", name: \"Collision\", parent: \"./Player\"},"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 320,
                  "content": "{op: \"SetProp\", path: \"./Player/Collision\", key: \"shape\", value: \"RectangleShape2D\"},"
                },
                {

                  "content": "{op: \"SetProp\", path: \"./Player/Collision\", key: \"shape\", value: \"RectangleShape2D\"},"
                },
                {

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 326,
                  "content": "## Complete 2D Scene Example"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 328,
                  "content": "Request: \"Create 2D platformer with ground, player, and camera\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 332,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"/\", \"type\": \"Node2D\", \"name\": \"World\"},"
                },
                {

                },
                {

                  "lineNumber": 339,

                {
                  "lineNumber": 341,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 346,
                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World/Player\", \"type\": \"CollisionShape2D\", \"name\": \"Collision\"},"
                },

                {

                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"AddNode\", \"parent\": \"./World\", \"type\": \"Camera2D\", \"name\": \"Camera\"},"
                },
                {

                  "content": "    {\"op\": \"SaveScene\", \"path\": \"res://main.tscn\"},"

                  "lineNumber": 355,
                  "content": "  \"reasoning\": \"Creating 2D platformer scene with ground platform, player character, and camera. Ground uses ColorRect for visual (800×20) with green color and StaticBody2D for collision with RectangleShape2D. Player uses CharacterBody2D with ColorRect visual (32×32, red) and CapsuleShape2D collision. Camera2D set as current. Scene saved and set as main scene.\","
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 356,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 361,
                  "content": "Scene Operations:"

                {
                  "lineNumber": 367,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 407,
                  "content": "- 2D uses Y-axis increasing downward (top = y=0)"
                }

Subagents docs are updated to match Summer’s behavior and stay high-level, without exposing internals.

**Removed (no secret sauce / prompt engineering):**
- Custom subagents section (file locations, YAML frontmatter, config fields)
- Full prompt examples (verifier, security-auditor)
- Bash and Browser built-ins (Cursor-specific; Summer doesn’t use them)
- Explicit invocation examples with subagent names
- Best practices that referenced prompts, descriptions, or config

**Aligned with Summer’s implementation:**
- **Explore** — read-only codebase exploration (grep, readFile, listTree), fast model
- **General purpose** — full write access, inherits orchestrator model
- **Expert queue** — domain experts for 3D and 2D scene creation
- Shell approval, grep search, Vercel infrastructure

**Kept Cursor-style:**
- High-level “what” and “why”
- When to use vs. not use
- No implementation details or prompt text

                {
                  "lineNumber": 9417,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"From our Cursor analysis: Cursor has 3 layers of user rules — global settings, &amp;#96;.cursorrules&amp;#96; file at project root, and &amp;#96;.cursor/rules/*.mdc&amp;#96; files for granular rules. All are injected into the AI's context as &amp;#96;&amp;lt;rules&amp;gt;&amp;#96; / &amp;#96;&amp;lt;user_rules&amp;gt;&amp;#96; tags in every message. See &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/master_system_prompt.md&amp;#96; Layer 3 for how Cursor injects them. Important finding: Cursor's subagents do NOT receive user rules — only the main agent does.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9425,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/07_RULES_SYSTEM.md&amp;#96; — Full spec\""
                },
                {
