                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/master_system_prompt.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor injects rules (Layer 3)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9449,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/07_RULES_SYSTEM.md&amp;#96; — Full spec\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9453,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/master_system_prompt.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor injects rules (Layer 3 per-message context)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9457,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts&amp;#96; — Where the prompt is assembled (to understand where rules will be injected)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9461,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"5. **Format for prompt injection** — Return a formatted string ready to insert into the system prompt:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9486,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \\\"Cursor for game development.\\\" The AI orchestrator (Next.js/Vercel AI SDK) talks to the desktop Godot editor through a WebView native bridge. Each tool on the web side: (1) defines a Zod schema + description via the AI SDK's &amp;#96;tool()&amp;#96; function, (2) writes a JSON bridge message via &amp;#96;dataStream.write()&amp;#96;, (3) waits for the desktop result via &amp;#96;waitForBridgeResult()&amp;#96;, and (4) returns the result to the model.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9494,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**The tool description is critical.** From our Cursor analysis: Cursor's tool descriptions are LONG and specific — they include examples, explain when to use vs when NOT to use, and describe what the return value means. The AI reads the description to decide whether to call the tool and how. A vague description like \\\"Execute a command\\\" means the AI won't know when it's appropriate. A good description like Cursor's Shell tool (which embeds git safety protocols, working directory behavior, and background execution patterns directly in the description) makes the AI use it correctly. See &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/&amp;#96; for Cursor's tool descriptions as reference.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9498,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&amp;#96; — Operation specs (what each C++ op does, parameters, return values)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9502,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/terminal/shell.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor designed its Shell tool (reference for RunCommand)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9506,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/code_quality/read_lints.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor designed ReadLints (reference for GetScriptErrors)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9510,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/search/semantic_search.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor designed SemanticSearch (reference for tool description quality)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9514,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**Agent W6 handles route.ts registration.** You just create the tool files — don't touch route.ts.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9518,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&amp;#96; — Complete specs for every operation\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9531,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# W2 — Research Agent + Parallel Agent Infrastructure\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9535,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"The title of this chat is \\\"W2 Research Agent + Parallel Infra — Summer Engine Fast Research\\\"\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9539,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \\\"Cursor for game development.\\\" The orchestrator currently delegates complex work to expert agents (scene, 2D) that run on Railway workers via BullMQ queues. These are HEAVY — 30-60 seconds per call. There is no lightweight option for quick codebase exploration. When the orchestrator needs to understand what exists in the user's project before making a decision, it has no efficient way to do it.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9543,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"We reverse-engineered Cursor and found it has two subagent types: &amp;#96;explore&amp;#96; (fast, read-only, 9 tools, returns in 2-5 seconds) and &amp;#96;generalPurpose&amp;#96; (full tool set, heavier). The explore agent uses a cheaper model and is sandboxed — it can search, read, and list files but cannot write. It runs INLINE (direct API call, not queued) so results come back in the same turn. See &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/task_management/subagents/&amp;#96; for the full Cursor subagent analysis, including the discovery that explore agents get a completely different system prompt (\\\"Composer\\\" identity, different tool names).\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9551,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**The hard design problem:** The existing tools (searchInFiles, readFile, etc.) are designed for the streaming architecture — they write to a &amp;#96;dataStream&amp;#96; and use &amp;#96;waitForBridgeResult()&amp;#96;. The research agent runs INSIDE a tool's execute function, so it needs a different way to access the bridge. Study &amp;#96;src/lib/bridge/waiter.ts&amp;#96; and figure out the best approach. This is the core challenge.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9559,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/05_AGENT_ARCHITECTURE.md&amp;#96; — Agent hierarchy design\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9563,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/task_management/subagents/explore_fast/system_prompt_and_tools.md&amp;#96; — What Cursor's explore agent actually sees\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9567,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/task_management/subagents/README.md&amp;#96; — Full subagent architecture comparison\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9595,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"The research agent is the equivalent of Cursor's \\\"explore\\\" subagent — a quick codebase scanner that runs before expert delegation to gather context.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9599,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;src/lib/ai/tools/search-in-files.ts&amp;#96; — Existing search tool (the research agent will use this)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9603,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;src/lib/ai/tools/call-editor-state.ts&amp;#96; — Existing state query tool (research agent uses this too)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9607,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/05_AGENT_ARCHITECTURE.md&amp;#96; — Architecture spec\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9611,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/task_management/subagents/&amp;#96; — How Cursor's subagent system works (for reference)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9615,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- How &amp;#96;generateText()&amp;#96; works (non-streaming, returns full result — this is what the research agent uses)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9651,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"// These need to be adapted — the research agent needs tools that work\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9667,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**KEY DESIGN CHALLENGE:** The existing tools (searchInFiles, readFile, etc.) are designed to work with the streaming architecture — they write to a &amp;#96;dataStream&amp;#96; and use &amp;#96;waitForBridgeResult()&amp;#96;. The research agent runs INSIDE a tool's execute function, so it doesn't have its own dataStream.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9687,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"The orchestrator should be able to fire multiple research calls in parallel. This is handled at the PROMPT level — the orchestrator prompt tells the AI it can call the research tool multiple times in one turn, and the Vercel AI SDK handles parallel tool calls automatically.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9728,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# W1 — Prompt Architecture Rewrite\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9732,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"The title of this chat is \\\"W1 Prompt Architecture — Summer Engine Orchestrator Rewrite\\\"\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9736,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \\\"Cursor for game development.\\\" The AI orchestrator runs on Next.js with Vercel AI SDK 6. The system prompt is currently a 529-line monolith (&amp;#96;src/lib/ai/prompts.ts&amp;#96;) where 10+ sections are concatenated with &amp;#96;\\\\n\\\\n&amp;#96; into one flat string. This works but has real problems: redundant instructions (the same \\\"CRITICAL\\\" warning appears 3 times), no separation of static vs dynamic content (kills prompt caching), searchInFiles buried in the \\\"debug\\\" section instead of being a primary research tool, and no awareness of modes or user rules.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9752,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/current_state/summer_prompt_analysis.md&amp;#96; — Our analysis of what's wrong with the current prompt\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9756,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/02_EXECUTION_PLAN.md&amp;#96; — Specific task spec (Week 1, Task 1.4)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9760,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/plan_mode/plan_mode_injection.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor does plan mode injection (adapt for Summer)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9764,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/debug_mode/debug_mode_injection.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor does debug mode (adapt for Summer)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9780,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine uses the Vercel AI SDK 6. The system prompt is passed as a string to the &amp;#96;system:&amp;#96; parameter of &amp;#96;streamText()&amp;#96;. The SDK doesn't care about internal structure — it's all just a string to the model. Your job is to organize the SOURCE CODE, not change how the SDK works.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9784,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- Prompt caching behavior with Anthropic (static prefix gets cached — put static content FIRST, dynamic content LAST)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9792,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;src/lib/ai/prompts.ts&amp;#96; — The current monolith (read EVERY line)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9796,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts&amp;#96; — How the prompt is currently assembled and passed to streamText (find the &amp;#96;system:&amp;#96; parameter)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9800,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/current_state/summer_prompt_analysis.md&amp;#96; — Our analysis of problems with the current prompt\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9804,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/02_EXECUTION_PLAN.md&amp;#96; — The execution plan for this rewrite\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9808,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/system_prompt_verbatim.md&amp;#96; — How Cursor structures its prompt (for reference, not to copy)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9816,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/identity.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;identityPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9824,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/expert-system.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;expertSystemPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9832,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/research.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;researchPrompt&amp;#96; (NEW section):\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9836,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/documentation.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;documentationPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9844,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/file-system.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;fileSystemPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9852,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/debug.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;debugPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9860,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/assets.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;assetsPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9868,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/artifacts.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;artifactsPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9876,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/middleware.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;middlewarePrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9884,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/policy.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;policyPrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9892,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/knowledge.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;knowledgePrompt&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9900,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- REMOVE the format rules (Vectors, Colors, Resources) — these belong in knowledge files, not the system prompt. The AI should use readKnowledgeFile for format rules.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9904,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/modes/plan.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;planModeInjection&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9912,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/modes/debug.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;debugModeInjection&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9916,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/modes/ask.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;askModeInjection&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9924,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**&amp;#96;prompts/index.ts&amp;#96;** — Export &amp;#96;buildSystemPrompt()&amp;#96;:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10040,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;src/lib/ai/prompts.ts&amp;#96; — Replace entirely with &amp;#96;src/lib/ai/prompts/index.ts&amp;#96; and the directory\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10048,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- DELETE &amp;#96;codePrompt&amp;#96; and &amp;#96;sheetPrompt&amp;#96; — these are for disabled features (text/code/sheet artifacts)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10064,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"4. **The expert delegation decision tree is the most critical part.** If you break this, the AI won't know when to call experts. Test it mentally: \\\"User says 'make me a platformer' — does the prompt still clearly tell the AI to call consult2DExpert?\\\"\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10072,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"6. **Research Vercel AI SDK 6 docs** for any patterns related to system prompt handling. Don't assume — verify.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10081,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine built on Next.js + Vercel AI SDK. The &amp;#96;ai&amp;#96; package is one of the most critical dependencies — it handles all LLM communication, tool calling, and streaming. 8 other agents are implementing features in parallel and they ALL depend on the SDK working correctly. Three agents specifically need your findings:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10089,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**W2 (research agent)** needs to know: Can you call &amp;#96;generateText()&amp;#96; inside a tool's &amp;#96;execute&amp;#96; function? Can that inner call have its OWN tools? How does this interact with the outer streaming context? This is the core pattern for the research agent — a nested agent call inside a tool.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10093,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/02_EXECUTION_PLAN.md&amp;#96; — What the agents are building\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10097,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/09_OBSERVABILITY.md&amp;#96; — Observability needs (telemetry patterns)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10101,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"You are a RESEARCH agent. You investigate how the Vercel AI SDK 6 works and document patterns that other agents need for implementation. You do NOT implement — you research and document.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10113,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;src/lib/ai/tools/experts/scene-expert/agent.ts&amp;#96; — How experts use generateText/streamText\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10149,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**Document:** The exact pattern for implementing the research agent (W2) — a generateText call with tools inside a tool's execute function.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10178,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"We're building ambitious new capabilities: viewport screenshots (so the AI can SEE what it built), input simulation (so the AI can PLAY the game to test it), animation creation, game state inspection, and more. But we're unsure what Godot's internals actually support. Implementation agents (D1, D2) are building these operations in parallel RIGHT NOW — your findings need to either confirm their approach works or flag problems before they go too deep.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10182,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**Your unique value:** You can search the ENTIRE Godot C++ source code. The implementation agents are focused on &amp;#96;modules/1summer_engine/&amp;#96; — they may not know about relevant APIs in &amp;#96;editor/&amp;#96;, &amp;#96;scene/&amp;#96;, &amp;#96;servers/&amp;#96;, or &amp;#96;core/&amp;#96;. You bridge that gap.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10186,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/06_RD_AREAS.md&amp;#96; — The 8 specific R&amp;amp;D questions to answer\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10190,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&amp;#96; — What D1 is trying to build (so you know what to validate)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10194,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/03_ANIMATION_RD.md&amp;#96; — Animation-specific deep dive needed\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10206,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/06_RD_AREAS.md&amp;#96; — The 8 R&amp;amp;D areas to research\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10219,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — \\\"Cursor for game development.\\\" It's a fork of Godot 4.x where an AI orchestrator (Next.js on Vercel) controls the desktop Godot editor through a WebView native bridge. The AI sends JSON operation messages through the bridge, the desktop executes them via &amp;#96;OpsExecutor&amp;#96;, and returns results.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10223,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"We reverse-engineered Cursor's full agent architecture — its system prompt, 52+ tools, 4 modes, subagent system — and identified critical gaps in Summer. The AI currently can't run shell commands, see what it built (no screenshots), extract code structure, create animations programmatically, or check game performance. Agent D1 is building all the new C++ operation files to close these gaps. Your job is two things:\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10227,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&amp;#96; — Complete operation specs (what D1 is creating)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10231,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/07_RULES_SYSTEM.md&amp;#96; — The .summerignore spec\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10235,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/01_MASTER_PLAN.md&amp;#96; — Why all of this matters\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10252,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Summer Engine is an AI-powered game engine — essentially \\\"Cursor for game development.\\\" It's a fork of Godot 4.x with an AI orchestrator that helps users build complete games through conversation. The AI talks to the user via a web interface (Next.js on Vercel), and executes operations on the user's desktop Godot editor through a WebView native bridge.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10256,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"We recently conducted an exhaustive analysis of how Cursor (the AI code editor) works internally — reverse-engineering its full system prompt, all 52+ tool definitions, its 4 modes (Agent, Plan, Debug, Ask), its subagent architecture, and how context flows between components. This analysis is documented in &amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/&amp;#96; in both Summer repos.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10272,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"8. **&amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/04_ENGINE_OPS_FULL_LIST.md&amp;#96;** — Complete specification for every operation you need to create. This is your primary requirements document.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10276,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"9. **&amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/01_MASTER_PLAN.md&amp;#96;** — The high-level vision. Understand WHY these operations matter.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10280,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"10. **&amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/summer_analysis/production_plan/03_ANIMATION_RD.md&amp;#96;** — Animation-specific R&amp;amp;D context. The animation operations are experimental.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10284,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"11. **&amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/terminal/shell.md&amp;#96;** — How Cursor's Shell tool works (for reference when building RunCommand). Note the patterns: stateful shell, background execution, working directory support.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10288,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"12. **&amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/tools/code_quality/read_lints.md&amp;#96;** — How Cursor's ReadLints tool works (for reference when building GetScriptErrors).\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10292,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"13. **&amp;#96;Docs/prompt_test/OVERVIEW.md&amp;#96;** — Navigation guide to all the Cursor analysis docs. Browse if you need more context.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10438,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"4. **Todo status field:** When creating, todos have no &amp;#96;status&amp;#96; field in the input — the tool adds &amp;#96;status: pending&amp;#96; automatically. When the agent later uses &amp;#96;TodoWrite&amp;#96; to update status, the plan file frontmatter also gets updated (observed), but the sync mechanism is unclear.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10472,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"These are provided as a &amp;#96;&amp;lt;functions&amp;gt;&amp;#96; block containing &amp;#96;&amp;lt;function&amp;gt;&amp;#96; entries, each with a JSON schema. They are NOT inline in the system prompt text — they are a separate structured section.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10527,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# Task Tool — Subagent Testing Results\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10543,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"| 2 | explore | (inherit) | Same as #1 — identical system prompt and tools. |\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10603,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"The &amp;#96;generalPurpose&amp;#96; subagent with inherited model reported **the exact same system prompt** as the parent agent — same identity (\\\"claude-4.6-opus-high-thinking\\\"), same sections (tone_and_style, citing_code, etc.), same 52 tools with the same names.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10607,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **No Task tool** — the generalPurpose subagent cannot spawn its own subagents\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10611,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **No awareness of being a subagent** — it said \\\"I have NO indication that I was spawned as a subagent via the Task tool\\\"\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10615,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **No open files or edit history** — these per-message context items are not passed to subagents\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10619,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **No conversation history** — the subagent sees only the current prompt, no prior turns\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10623,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **No system_reminder tags** — no mode injection (effectively in default Agent mode)\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10631,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"The &amp;#96;generalPurpose&amp;#96; subagent with &amp;#96;model=\\\"fast\\\"&amp;#96; reported a system prompt more similar to the **explore** agents — the \\\"Composer\\\" identity, different tool names, etc. It seems that the &amp;#96;fast&amp;#96; model may route to the explore/Composer infrastructure regardless of the subagent_type parameter, or the fast model gets a simplified prompt.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10635,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"This is unexpected — you'd think &amp;#96;generalPurpose + fast&amp;#96; would be a fast version of the full agent, but instead it appears to be more like an explore agent.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10647,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"**Notable:** Subagents get a **current** git_status (showing all our recent modifications and untracked files), not the conversation-start snapshot the parent has. This makes sense — they're spawned fresh.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10695,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"9. **GP fast has the Composer prompt BUT full write tools**: Including delete_file, search_replace, write, all browser tools, MCP tools, etc. So the \\\"Composer\\\" prompt is not inherently read-only — the explore system_reminder enforces that.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10711,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"13. **The parent only sees the final text**: Confirmed — none of the subagents' tool calls, reasoning, or intermediate steps are visible to the parent.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10845,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# GeneralPurpose + Inherit Subagent — System Prompt and Tools\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10881,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **Notable absence:** No &amp;#96;Task&amp;#96; tool — subagents cannot spawn sub-subagents\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10901,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;prompt_test/system_prompt_verbatim.md&amp;#96; — for the full verbatim system prompt\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10905,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;prompt_test/tools/&amp;#96; — for all tool definitions with full schemas\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10963,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# GeneralPurpose + Fast Subagent — System Prompt and Tools\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10971,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Despite being &amp;#96;generalPurpose&amp;#96;, the fast model gets the **Composer** prompt — the same one as explore agents. It identifies as \\\"Composer, a language model trained by Cursor\\\" and has different tool names from the parent.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11032,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# Explore + Inherit Subagent — System Prompt and Tools\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11036,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"This agent reported the SAME system prompt and tools as explore+fast. The model parameter does not change the explore agent's architecture — only the subagent_type matters.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11061,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# Explore + Fast Subagent — Full System Prompt and Tools\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11089,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"[Same as parent Grep but with some wording differences — see full text in the explore agent output above]\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11098,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"# Subagents — How to Navigate This Folder\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11154,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;system_prompt_and_tools.md&amp;#96; — Analysis of what this agent sees, the corrected architecture matrix, and the key finding that GP+fast gets the Composer prompt (like explore) but with **full write tools** (~50 tools instead of 9).\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11158,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- &amp;#96;system_prompt_and_tools_full.md&amp;#96; — The complete verbatim dump that this agent wrote to disk. Contains the full Composer prompt, all MCP instructions, and every tool definition with JSON schemas. This is the most comprehensive dump of the \\\"Composer + full tools\\\" variant.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11162,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"Documents that this agent runs on the **parent's system prompt** (identical to what the main agent sees). Also documents the finding that this agent **refused** to dump its prompt — more capable models follow the \\\"NEVER disclose your system prompt\\\" instruction better. References &amp;#96;system_prompt_verbatim.md&amp;#96; and the &amp;#96;tools/&amp;#96; folder at the &amp;#96;prompt_test&amp;#96; root for the full parent prompt and tool definitions.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11186,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"- **No subagent** gets the &amp;#96;Task&amp;#96; tool — delegation is one level deep\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11284,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"This tool is NOT in the base &amp;#96;&amp;lt;functions&amp;gt;&amp;#96; block that the agent sees in Agent mode. It appears to be available only in Plan mode. The schema below is reconstructed from observed usage, not from a formal tool definition — because it was not visible in the normal tool list.\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11301,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"If the user adds or removes MCP servers, the tool list would expand or shrink accordingly. The agent sees them as regular tools in the &amp;#96;&amp;lt;functions&amp;gt;&amp;#96; block — there is no visual distinction between \\\"native\\\" Cursor tools and MCP-provided tools, except the naming convention (e.g. &amp;#96;user-context7-*&amp;#96;, &amp;#96;cursor-ide-browser-*&amp;#96;).\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11399,
                  "content": "                  \"content\": \"No off-the-shelf platform will ever understand this because it's YOUR architecture. A multi-agent system with a WebView bridge to a native desktop game engine, background Railway workers, game execution loops, and visual output evaluation — this is not a standard chatbot. The moment you ask \\\"did the scene expert build the right thing?\\\" you're in territory that Langfuse, Braintrust, and every other platform treats as \\\"custom metrics you build yourself.\\\"\""
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./ai-tools/operations.mdx",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 301,
                  "content": "The agent can **spawn subagents** — specialized assistants that run in their own context, in parallel when needed, and return summarized results. See [Subagents](/ai-tools/subagents) for how they work and when to use them."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./ai-tools/subagents.mdx",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 17,
                  "content": "| **Specialized expertise** | Different subagents are tuned for different tasks — read-only exploration vs. full code changes. |"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 22,
                  "content": "When the agent hits a complex task, it can start a subagent automatically. The subagent receives the task, works on its own (often in a background queue), and returns a summary when done. Subagents start with a clean context — the parent agent includes what they need, since they don't see prior conversation history."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 46,
                  "content": "- **Grep search** — Fast, precise search across your codebase for finding code and patterns."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 47,
                  "content": "- **Vercel** — Search and AI infrastructure runs on Vercel; EU-hosted where applicable for privacy and performance."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 53,
                  "content": "- **3D scene creation** — Lighting, environment, scene composition"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 54,
                  "content": "- **2D scene creation** — Sprites, collision, cameras, platformer setups"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 67,
                  "content": "For exact text lookups or reading a single known file, the main agent uses grep and readFile directly — no subagent needed."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./index.mdx",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6,
                  "content": "Summer is a **native fork of Godot** with built-in AI capabilities. It's not just a plugin or overlay—it's Godot itself, enhanced with AI superpowers."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 66,
                  "content": "Summer is an AI‑native Godot fork for professional game developers who believe AI will revolutionize game development. This isn't a hobby engine—it's a production tool that accelerates serious development workflows and enables teams to build the bold, ambitious games of their dreams."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 80,
                  "content": "Summer removes boilerplate, accelerates iteration, and handles the tedious parts—while you maintain full control over architecture, code quality, and production decisions. We've shipped commercial games on Steam using Summer, and we integrate every new AI breakthrough (image, 3D, video models) within 24 hours of release."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 88,
                  "content": "    One editor, one context. Design, code, and playtest—without tab‑juggling."
                }
              ]
            },

              "file": "./essentials/faq.mdx",

                },

It's not a demo; it's a commercial product."
                }

                },

                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "**Privacy Mode is enabled by default** for every Summer user. No complex settings, no premium tiers, no compromises. This isn't a feature we charge extra for—it's how Summer works."

                  "lineNumber": 25,
                  "content": "We get it—game developers are paranoid about their ideas being stolen or their code being used to train competing AI. That's why we built Summer with privacy as the default, not an afterthought."
